dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to a patient with breathing difficulties reported on the curb.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain and shortness of breath started three days ago and have gotten worse today.
Would you say it started suddenly or has it gradually gotten worse?
It slowly worsened over three days, and the last 20 minutes were the most intense.
Let me check your alertness - can you tell me your name and the date today?
I am John, and today is May 26, 2020.
Good, you're alert and oriented. How are you feeling now?
I feel alert but very uncomfortable.
On a scale of 1 to 15, that would make you around a 15 - excellent orientation.
Let's assess your airway - can I have a look at your throat for any signs of blockage?
Airway is clear and patent, no signs of obstruction present.
Your breathing is increased in rate at 39 breaths per minute.
You have a wet-sounding cough with minimal increased work of breathing observed.
Pulse is strong and regular at 88 beats per minute.
Skin is warm, dry, with normal capillary refill and no signs of cyanosis.
Your heart rate is 88.
Respirations are at 39 per minute.
Blood pressure is 138 over 83 mmHg.
Your oxygen saturation is 95%.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm with no arrhythmias.
Let's go further into your chest pain. Was it sharp or dull?
It is sharp and located in the middle of my chest, 8 out of 10.
Does it get worse with activity or deep breaths?
Yes, deep breaths and movement intensify it a bit.
Any nausea, vomiting, or chills alongside this?
I have had some chills and nausea over the past few days.
I see from the report that you have a history of COPD and bronchitis. How are those managed now?
I had pneumonia two weeks ago and was prescribed medications, but they were stolen.
Are you aware of any recent contact with someone who had or suspected to have COVID-19?
No, I don't know of any recent contacts.
You mentioned the chest pain comes and goes - when exactly did it start?
The pain has been gradual, but the last 20 minutes were the worst.
What, if anything, eases the pain?
Sitting up slightly helps, but deep breaths make it worse.
How would you describe the pain other than being sharp - burning or aching?
It's more felt than anything else - sharp.
Does it travel to your arms, back, or jaw?
No, it stays in the middle of my chest.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how bad is it now?
It's still at 8, but a little improved since the medication.
Can you recall when the pain first happened and how long it's lasted?
It started about three days ago and has been progressively worse.
Let me examine your skin - how does your temperature feel?
Skin is warm to the touch with no abnormal rashes or color changes.
I'll now check your pupils and neck for any issues.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive at 3 mm each.
No swelling, deformities, or signs of jugular venous distention noted.
Checking your throat for signs of congestion or fluid secretions.
No drainage or obstructions noted in the airway.
Breathing sounds are present bilaterally with mild rhonchi.
No asymmetry in chest movements or signs of wheezing observed.
Trachea is centered, and breathing effort remains mild without sign of labored breathing.
Let's assess your abdomen - any pain or swelling present?
No significant findings - no abdominal tenderness, rigidity, or visible scars noted.
Can you move your arms and legs freely?
Yes, I can move my arms and legs without issue.
No signs of swelling, deformities, or use issues observed.
Blood glucose can't be assessed at this time - no necessary findings for immediate concern.
We'll continue to monitor closely for any changes or concerns.
Your ongoing EKG remains stable and no new changes from the initial results observed.
I'll be starting an IV now to support future medication or fluid administration if needed.
IV successfully placed in your left antecubital vein with no signs of complications.
We've applied a 4-lead ECG monitor for continuous cardiac monitoring per protocol.
Capnography is on and showing mild tachypnea with normocapnia and a sharkfin end-tidal waveform.
I'm now giving you 10 mg dexamethasone IV to help reduce inflammation.
Additionally, I'm giving you 0.25 mg terbutaline SQ to improve your breathing. Do you have any concerns?
No, I understand and accept the treatment.
You will be placed in a semi-Fowler's position with lap and shoulder belts for transport.
I feel fine in this position - no issues with the positioning.
We are transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further assessment and care.
Hospital has accepted you for transport - you'll be sent to room 5 for initial medical evaluation.
Pulse is 82, respirations 29, BP is 157 over 96, and O2 sat is 96%.
Rechecking IV site - it's patent, still very clean, and secure.
Your pain now, since your last check, remains at 8 or so?
Slightly better - now around 8 but still hurts with deep breaths.
Any changes in your symptoms or new discomfort since we began?
No - it's steady and doesn't radiate or get worse with movement.
You mentioned having schizoaffective disorder - currently, are you experiencing dizziness or visual changes?
No, I've had no dizziness or visual disturbances recently.
Feeling stable for transport? Any numbing or unusual chest discomfort now?
I feel okay - no other symptoms so far.
The hospital has been alerted to your condition, medications administered, and vitals.
12-lead EKG and capnography results were shared with receiving hospital for pre-arrival preparation.
Equipment is secured, and EKG and capnography are ready for transit.
Let's ensure we have everything secure for transport - all baseline vitals updated.
We are following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress - all steps are normal as per protocol.
[COMPANY_NAME] is ready to receive you now - no changes needed in care plans or monitoring.
[COMPANY_NAME] receiving nurse is aware and visually ready during our arrival process.
Stretcher is ready for movement - all monitoring systems verified and locked.
We will continue to monitor and adjust treatment if needed during the short ride.
All vitals, medications, and assessments are ready for transition and handoff to [COMPANY_NAME] medical team.
Final updates on vital signs and imaging have been transmitted for [COMPANY_NAME]'s standing protocols.
Movement is updated - no new concerns, all vitals stable for the trip ahead.
Before we proceed, can you rate your pain again? Improved since administration?
Still 8 out of 10 - it's managing, but not gone.
We'll keep EKG and capnography working for continued monitoring.
Protocol 3-11 managed as outlined - no deviation noticed from our intervention or patient flow.
Equipment locked, patient stable, and on our way! Let's make this final check.
Let me know if you notice worsening symptoms - improvements are good, but we stay ready.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the destination. Transfer will be smooth - final prep underway.
All protocol steps accounted for and confirmed for [COMPANY_NAME] prearrival expectations.
We are the last to confirm - stable and arriving on time.
All set - take a deep breath. Ready when they are at the hospital. Let's proceed!
